name: Begonia darthvaderiana
aboutText: Begonia darthvaderiana, described by C.W.Lin and C.I.Peng, is a rare species endemic to the ultramatic rainforest understory of Sabah in northern Borneo — one of the most botanically isolated and species-rich environments on earth. It grows in warm, very humid, low-light conditions on mossy forest-floor substrate rich in leaf litter and organic matter. The plant's unmistakable bat-wing foliage is its entire identity: large, asymmetric leaves of dark olive to near-black with a slightly leathery, glossy surface and a winged, dramatically silhouetted outline. Deep red, densely hairy petioles anchor each leaf. A creeping rhizome at substrate level produces fibrous roots and successive leaves along the stem. Named for the immediately obvious visual allusion, darthvaderiana has become one of the most coveted Bornean Begonias in collector circles worldwide.

morphology.leafShape: Asymmetric, bat-wing outline with winged silhouette
morphology.venation: Visible on slightly glossy dark blade
morphology.texture: Slightly leathery, glossy, dark olive to near-black
morphology.growthHabit: Creeping rhizome producing fibrous roots and new leaves along stem

propagation.notes: Most rhizomatous begonias root readily from leaf sections or petiole cuttings placed on moist substrate. Keep warm and humid until plantlets emerge at leaf margins.

careGuide.substrate: Light, well-draining mix: 50% perlite, 30% potting compost, 20% orchid bark. Begonias are susceptible to root rot — excellent drainage is essential.
careGuide.watering: Allow the top half of the substrate to dry between waterings. Water at the base — wet leaves invite fungal problems. Reduce in winter.
careGuide.humidity: 50–70%. More tolerant of average indoor humidity than tropical aroids, but consistent moisture prevents crispy leaf margins.
careGuide.fertilising: Balanced liquid fertiliser at quarter strength every 2–3 weeks during the growing season. Do not feed in winter.
careGuide.repotting: Every 12–18 months in spring. Begonias like shallow, wide pots that suit rhizomatous growth. Don't bury the rhizome.

commonProblems.cause: Poor air circulation combined with high humidity
commonProblems.fix: Improve airflow; avoid wetting leaves; treat with a dilute bicarbonate of soda spray

commonProblems.cause: Overwatering, dead leaves left on plant, poor air circulation
commonProblems.fix: Remove dead foliage promptly; increase airflow; reduce watering
Move to a brighter position — begonias need good indirect light to maintain compact growth

items: Check for powdery mildew — a white dusty coating on leaf surfaces, especially in dry conditions; it spreads rapidly between plants
Inspect for mealybugs at leaf axils and the underside of stems where they hide in white cottony deposits
Rhizome begonias should show firm, healthy rhizome tissue — avoid plants with soft, discoloured rhizomes
Most begonias ship well as leaf cuttings — if buying a cutting, ensure it has a healthy petiole and no signs of rot at the cut end

fieldNotes.title: Named with Intent
fieldNotes.author: Aroid Aaron
fieldNotes.content: Formally described in 2017, Begonia darthvaderiana is named — with some botanical wit — for Darth Vader, on account of the extremely dark, near-black leaf surface that the species produces on the ultramatic (serpentinite-derived) soils of its Sabah habitat. These nickel-rich soils produce distinctively stressed and often dark-leafed plants across multiple taxa; the darkness is a physiological response to toxic soil chemistry as much as anything aesthetic. At accessible pricing, perhaps worth the challenge for the collector who enjoys a difficult substrate and a good story. The name also never fails to produce a reaction at plant shows.
